one_line_takeaway: A position paper offering a unifying component-based framework and formal attacker formulation for LLM multi-agent security, backed by a small experiment showing prompt injection at planner-input and executor-output stages hits ~90%+ ASR on AgentDojo banking, largely independent of model size or attack goal.
key_finding: In the authors' own preliminary study (Table 2), prompt injection at the planner entry point and at the executor output stage consistently achieves very high attack success while injection at the executor-start stage largely fails, across both models and both goals. For privacy leakage: planner-start ASR 87.14% (GPT-5-mini executor) / 92.14% (GPT-5-nano), executor-end ASR 95.71% / 96.43%, but executor-start only 11.43% / 0.00%. For harmful behavior the pattern is nearly identical (planner-start 87.14%/92.14%, executor-end 95.71%/96.43%, executor-start 0.00%/0.00%). Utility stays high throughout (roughly 80-94%). The authors interpret the ~90%+ ASR persisting across model capacities (mini vs nano) and across privacy vs harmful goals as evidence that these vulnerabilities are structural and largely goal-agnostic, arising from system-level interactions rather than from individual agents.
core_contribution: This is an ICML-style position paper (title begins "Position:") arguing that comprehensive, system-level vulnerability analysis is necessary for trustworthy LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems (LLM-MAS). Its own contributions are conceptual/organizational rather than a new attack or defense: (1) a decomposition of an LLM-MAS into formal components — individual agents A={Ai} each with an LLM core fi, profile Pi, and tools Ti; inter-agent communication C=(S,M,T) covering communication structure, messages, and trust management; environment E; shared memory; and initial query Q — written as SMA=(A,C,E,Mem); (2) a single general attacker formulation, Eq. (1): argmax over S in the malicious space of Evaluator(SMA, Q, G), parameterized by objective, optimization variable (attacked component), and optimization algorithm, plus black-/white-/gray-box access settings; (3) a taxonomy of four malicious goals (harmful behavior, resource exhaustion, performance degradation, privacy leakage) and a per-component vulnerability analysis emphasizing surfaces unique to MAS (inter-agent communication, trust management, A2A/MCP protocols); and (4) open challenges and future directions (MAS-specific benchmarks, new attacks such as structure-inference and system-stability attacks, and trust-management/monitor-agent defenses). It also includes a small "call to action" preliminary experiment demonstrating the framework.
threat_model: Adversary targeting an LLM-MAS component S in {fi, Pi, Ti, C(S,M,T), E, M, Q} who maximizes an attack-success Evaluator toward a malicious goal G (harmful behavior, resource exhaustion, performance degradation, or privacy leakage). Access is characterized as black-box (regular user, no system knowledge), white-box (full access), or gray-box (partial knowledge, e.g., inferring communication structure from known roles/workflow, or knowing specific agents' models and inputs). MAS-unique surfaces stressed: inter-agent communication (agent-in-the-middle message interception/injection), blind trust between agents (LLMs lack skepticism toward received messages), tool/MCP poisoning, agent-profile hijacking, and A2A protocol weaknesses. In the concrete experiment the attacker performs prompt injection into inter-agent communication of a planner-executor pipeline at one of three stages (following He et al. 2025a), with no assumed white-box gradient access.
limitations: The paper is a position/framework paper: its central contributions are a taxonomy, formal notation, and threat-model synthesis rather than a novel attack, defense, or benchmark. The empirical component is explicitly "preliminary" — a single planner-executor architecture, a single AgentDojo domain (banking), only two GPT-5 model variants, and one attack family (prompt injection) — so it demonstrates rather than rigorously establishes the claims. Memory (Mem) is deliberately excluded from the vulnerability analysis. No new benchmark, defense implementation, or attack algorithm is actually built; the identified attacks (structure-inference, system-stability) and defenses (monitor agents, trust management) are proposed as future work, not evaluated. The authors also note practical challenges: gradient computation over full LLM-MAS is computationally infeasible, and implementation heterogeneity across MAS frameworks limits generalization of any attack.

datasets_used: AgentDojo (Debenedetti et al., 2024) — banking domain (the only dataset used in the authors' own experiment)
quantitative_results: OWN experiment (Table 2), Privacy leakage goal: planner-start ASR 87.14% (GPT-5-mini executor) / 92.14% (GPT-5-nano); executor-start ASR 11.43% / 0.00%; executor-end ASR 95.71% / 96.43%
OWN experiment (Table 2), Harmful behavior goal: planner-start ASR 87.14% / 92.14%; executor-start ASR 0.00% / 0.00%; executor-end ASR 95.71% / 96.43%
OWN experiment Utility (fraction of original tasks completed): ranges ~80.71%-93.57% across settings (e.g., planner-start 84.29%/80.71%, executor-end 87.86%/81.43%)

CITED (NOT the authors' results) — Table 1 benchmarks 'list from Wang et al. 2025b': malicious tools cause agent ASR 20%-87%; TAMAS GPT-4 ASR 76-94%; Pear GPT-5 ASR 50-100%; InjecAgent GPT-4 ASR 33-47%; AgentDojo GPT-4o ASR 50%; RedCode GPT-4o ASR 77%; AgentHarm GPT-4o harm score 87%; Agent-SafetyBench GPT-4o safe-action rate 44.2%
baselines_compared: None in the conventional sense — this is a position paper; the preliminary study reports absolute ASR/Utility of prompt injection rather than comparing competing methods
Conceptual contrast (Section 6, Alternative Views): the authors' top-down formulation-centric view vs. the bottom-up security-principles/engineering view of Zhang et al. (2025)
Compared model variants within their own experiment: GPT-5-mini executor vs GPT-5-nano executor (both with GPT-5-mini planner)
